summary: The post argues that indie games going open source usually release only code, not assets, and that this leaves the most interesting creative material inaccessible. Ronacher explains that open sourcing code makes sense for collaboration and community goodwill, but for games it rarely benefits typical players or commercial developers because GPL terms and game-specific engines limit reuse. He notes that the economic reality of indie development makes full asset releases risky, since assets are what most players value. The deeper issue is cultural: programmers embrace open source, artists generally do not, and that shapes what gets shared. He concludes by urging experimentation with opening assets or music—perhaps even instead of code—as a way to test new models without destroying revenue.
key_points: Open sourcing games typically means code-only releases, which are valuable for learning but less so for players or commercial reuse.
The motivations for open sourcing and for using open source often diverge, and games expose this mismatch.
GPL licensing and game-specific engines make open-sourced game code impractical for commercial derivatives or console ports.
Indie economics and high piracy rates make full asset releases financially risky, even if they might increase trust.
The real barrier is cultural: programmers favor open source, while artists rarely do, which limits asset sharing.
money_quotes: Which I personally can totally understand but I find it a little bit sad.
Mainly I find it sad because I don't care too much about the sourcecode.
In an ideal world they could open source everything, but that won't work in practice.
No sane developer that wants to sell software would attempt to base his game on a GPL software when there are no commercial licensing terms available.
The core issue here however is not that the assets are special: the assets are not more special than the code is.
Programmers love Open Source, Artists not so much.
I am quite sure that with a carefully crafted license one could still sell the game and also have it under an Open Source-ish license.
key_insight: The real obstacle to truly open-source games isn’t technical or legal so much as cultural—code gets shared because programmers value openness, while assets stay closed because artists and indie economics make that openness feel risky.
